text.text: ().

### Prerequisites

In this text, we will explore, among other topics, knowledge about the relationship of our species with others, as well as the expansion of the Universe.

Therefore, we suggest studying the first chapter of the *Atlas* (<htcite0>), as well as the text about humans (<htcite1>), available on History Tracers, before proceeding.

### Introduction

Since we use calendars daily, we often do not pay attention to how they were created and, above all, how they are used.

In this text, we wish to draw attention to some details that often lead people to conclusions that, although they do not represent their own realities, give the impression of being the "only" conclusions. To make this even clearer, we will begin with a reflection.

Reflection 1: The periods

Archaeological and anthropological findings indicate that we, Homo sapiens, had a common origin in Africa approximately 300,000 years ago (). Does this mean that we all use the same calendar?

Does that calendar have the same starting point? In other words, are we all in the year 300,000?

Why should we consider the emergence of our species as the starting date? Why not consider the beginning of the Universe, which occurred nearly 14 billion years ago (Figure 1) ()?
Figure 1: The universe through space-time ().

### The Closest Beginning (South of Abya Yala)

Throughout our lives, we have to deal with different periods of time. The period closest to us is that of our own life. Within it, our beginning is marked by birth, and from that day onward, we keep a count to know our age.

Now, when we live in a society, we need to look beyond ourselves and seek something that helps the entire community to organize itself.

To better understand this scenario, we will initially study cultures and empires from the south of America (*Abya Yala*), because the choices made by these peoples do not differ greatly from the choices made by other peoples.

#### The Incas

The information we have about the Inca calendar was initially recorded by Spanish chroniclers who, apparently, could not determine whether they were dealing with a solar or lunar calendar. It is very possible that, when writing their chronicles, they omitted information related to lunar cycles, producing a description more aligned with their own worldview (<htcite0>).

This difficulty in determining whether there was a solar, lunar, or lunisolar calendar was also highlighted by Elena Ortiz García when analyzing different chronicles from the Spanish colonial period (<htcite1>).

Furthermore, she presents another piece of information relevant to understanding the Incas and their calendars. According to her work, it would have been the Sapa Inca Pachacuti (**Figure 2**) who was responsible for implementing this system (<htcite2>). For this reason, when you select the Inca calendar in the **Date format** field, the years are counted from the estimated period of the beginning of his rule, in <htdate0>.
Figure 2: Statue of Pachacuti in the city of Cuzco (). Photo taken on , during a visit to the city.

source.text: Sitios del Patrimonio Mundial del Perú

text.imgdesc: Pachacuti is depicted atop a two-tiered fountain, holding a scepter in his right hand that reaches the ground. His left hand is raised as if pointing toward a path, with all fingers extended.

text.text:
The relationship between the solar calendar and the Inca Empire (*Tawantinsuyu*) is reinforced by the existence of pillars around Cusco (<htcite0>), with the beginning of the year occurring at what would correspond to the summer solstice — the longest day of the year in the Southern Hemisphere — on December 21 (<htcite1>). This date is associated with the beginning of the new annual cycle in various interpretations of historical records (<htcite2>).

This relationship can also be observed from the work of Felipe Guaman Poma de Ayala (<htcite3>).
Livro 1: Página 1139 da obra de Felipe Guaman Poma de Ayala.

#### A Cultural Complexity

Among the peoples who occupied the south of the American (*Abya Yala*) continent, the Incas and the *Tawantinsuyu* Empire are probably the best known. This fact leads us to a reflection:

Reflection 2: Greater and unique?

When there is a dominant empire, with ideas about how the world works that differ from those of other peoples, does that mean that only it possesses a valid understanding of the facts because it became larger?

If, by chance, your conclusion to this reflection was "yes," it is likely that throughout your life you have used information coming from a limited group of sources, which can make it difficult to see perspectives beyond those that have been presented to you.

Currently, there are billions of people in the world, with diverse cultures. Even so, culture is often still understood through ideas formulated about 250 years ago, which are not aligned with the contemporary understanding of this concept (<htcite0>).

This complexity led researchers to define the term "biocultural" to express the relationships of human beings among themselves and also with other species (<htcite1>). Using this concept, we can study other peoples from the south of the American (*Abya Yala*) continent who have calendars different from those of the Incas.

#### Biocultural Calendars

When we observe the Mapuche and Aymara peoples, we can notice that both use calendars whose starting point is the winter solstice — the shortest day of the year in the Southern Hemisphere — (<htcite2>). However, while the Mapuche celebrate the New Year, the *We tripantu*, between June 21 and 24, according to the Gregorian calendar, the Aymara celebrate the *Machaq mara* on June 21 (<htcite3>).

### A beginning for everyone

So far in this text, we have studied the beginning of a year for the peoples of South America (*Abya Yala*), which leads us to one more reflection before we proceed:

Reflection 3: Here, there, and everywhere

The peoples of the Andes used the Sun and the Moon in their calendars.

Do you understand that other peoples also used the Sun, the Moon, and the seasons to create their calendars?

After the New Year period between June 21 and 24, We tripantu, besides marking the beginning of winter in the Southern Hemisphere, also initiates a new period in which exposure to the Sun begins to gradually increase, allowing for agriculture and fishing activities ().

This fact — that the Sun starts to be visible for longer — could it also have affected people's lives beyond just food?

The fact that there are different lengths of days and nights during the solstices, with the shortest days in the South occurring in June, while in the North they occur in December (), could it have created similar understandings, but with a six-month interval between peoples?

#### My beginning is not your beginning

Due to the fact that the seasons are not the same across the entire planet, it would not be expected that a common calendar, with a common beginning, would exist. This can be observed by comparing today's date in the calendars presented by History Tracers (**Table 1**):

CalendarToday's date
Aymara
Chinese
Gregorian
Hebrew
Hispanic
Inca
Islamic
Japanese
Javanese
Julian
Mapuche
Mesoamerican
Extended Mesoamerican
Persian
French Revolution
Shaka
Table 1: Current date in different calendars.

The wide variation in dates among calendars helps us observe some important points:
- The years — generally the last number shown in the calendars — have different counts, even though the period Earth takes to orbit the Sun is the same for all of humanity.
- The days are also not necessarily the same, indicating that the subdivisions of time are different in most cases. This fact can be explained because some calendars are solar and others are lunar.

These human data, which relate to nature, although often implicit, also reveal that, as human knowledge advanced, we ended up maintaining calendars that tend to distance us from nature and, consequently, from the understanding of natural phenomena.

#### An equal beginning for everyone (Evolution)

If we consider only human relations, there are different scenarios we can choose to define a common calendar. Let us look again at the cladogram of our species (**Graph 1**):
Graph 1: Species related to ours over time.

An analysis of **Graph 1**, together with data from different research studies and fields of knowledge, shows that if we want a common year for our species, we would all currently be in the year 300,000 (<htcite0>; <htcite1>), unless new research finds older fossils.

That is, we would have to work with years that can change as knowledge evolves, which leads us to reflect once more.

Reflection 4: Convenience and reality

Reality is complex. To understand it, years of study are required. Given this scenario:

Would it be better to maintain traditions or to create a calendar based on measured and verifiable data?

Would a calendar based on precise measurements also accept having its start date changed according to the advancement of knowledge? Would that be easily understood?

How to balance tradition and scientific knowledge?

#### A physical beginning

The understanding of the evolution of the Universe (**Figure 1**) has also been constructed over time, as shown in **Table 2**:

|Discovery|Description|Sources|
|------------|------------|--------------|
|Age of the Universe (<htdate0>)|Updated to 13,82 billion years|<htcite0>|
|Size of the Universe (<htdate1>)|Updated to 94 billion light-years.|<htcite1>|
|Size of the Universe (<htdate2>)|Updated to 30 billion light-years.|<htcite2>|
|Size of the Universe (<htdate3>)|Updated to 25 billion light-years.| <htcite3>; <htcite4>|
|CMB (<htdate4>)|Confirmation of the first radiation in the universe.|<htcite5>|
|Size of the Universe (<htdate5>)|Updated to 4 billion light-years.|<htcite6> ; <htcite7>|
|Expanding Universe (<htdate6>)|Evidence that the universe is not static.|<htcite8>|
|Size of the Universe (<htdate7>)|Updated to 280 million light-years.|<htcite9>|
|Size of the Universe (<htdate8>)|Updated to 300,000 light-years.|<htcite10>|
|Theory of Relativity (<htdate9>)|Revolutionized the perception of space, time, and gravity.|<htcite11>|
|Why the Sky is Blue (<htdate10>)|Light scattering in the atmosphere creates the perception of a blue sky.|<htcite12>|
|Observation of a Nebula (<htdate11>)|First observation of a stellar nursery.|<htcite13>|
|Planetary Motion (<htdate12>)|Explanation of planetary movements.|<htcite14>|
|Earth Is Not the Center (<htdate13>)|Demonstrated that Earth is not at the center of the universe.|<htcite15>|
|Earth’s Size Confirmed (<htdate14> - <htdate15>)|Accurate calculation of Earth’s size through empirical measurement.|<htcite16>|
|Earth is Spherical (<htdate16>)|Demonstrated Earth’s spherical shape.|<htcite17>|
|Heaven as a Superior Level (<htdate17> - <htdate18>)|Sumerian texts describe heaven as an inhabitable region.|<htcite18> ;<htcite19> ;  <htcite20> ; <htcite21>|

source.text: Penzias, A. A., & Wilson R. W.
Gilgamesh and the Bull of Heaven
Gilgamesh and the Bull of Heaven

source.page: Gilgamesh and the Bull of Heaven

text.text: Table 2: The expansion of knowledge about the sky and the Earth.

Given this scenario, we also cannot affirm that, by defining a year based on what we know today, we will not need to revise it in the future. If we carry out more precise measurements and make new discoveries, we may once again recalculate the age of the Universe.

On the other hand, by using the age of the Universe as a starting point, we would have a common temporal reference for all living beings, reinforcing the understanding, present among the indigenous peoples of *Abya Yala*, of our connections with nature (<htcite0>).

Reflection 5: Isn't it too big?

The solution of understanding our time from the age of the Universe solves many problems related to timekeeping and the understanding of the cosmos, but it also raises some important questions:

The age of approximately 13.8 billion years is a much larger value than any date we currently use. How would the general population accept this change?

Do people understand how we know the Universe is that old?

Would they know which instruments we use and how to interpret their results?

Or would they prefer to continue using calendars that, although they may not be fully connected to measurable facts, are at least based on ideas they already manage to understand?

### Conclusion

In this text, we learned that the seasons of the year are determined according to Earth's positioning in relation to the Sun.

We also saw that the seasons were used by different peoples to create their calendars. These peoples took the winter solstice — the day with the least sun exposure — as a starting point and, as sun exposure increased, they carried out activities such as agriculture and fishing, thus developing their ways of life.

Finally, we observed that, despite our current knowledge about the origins of our species and the workings of the universe, a new adaptation of calendars using this data would face some problems, as new discoveries could require changes in the counting of years.

exercise_v2.question: Is there a possibility of considering the Inca calendar a solar calendar?
exercise_v2.additionalInfo: An analysis of the chronicles leads us to the conclusion that this possibility exists, but it is also possible to find chronicles that indicate it is lunar.

exercise_v2.question: Do all the peoples of the South commemorate their calendars on the summer solstices, on December 21?
exercise_v2.additionalInfo: The Incas commemorated the beginning of their year on the summer solstice, but other peoples, such as the Mapuches and the Aymaras, began their years during the winter solstice.

exercise_v2.question: Does a calendar that begins on a winter solstice help mark planting and fishing seasons, contributing to the organization of life?
exercise_v2.additionalInfo: By observing nature, different cultures created calendars that began on the day with the least sunlight — the winter solstice. In this way, they were able to better organize agricultural activities, ensuring food for the future. In other words, it is as if this new year marked the beginning of a new life cycle.
